items.title: Neuron budget / daily cap pattern
items.summary: Technique for staying under a free-tier GPU quota — track cumulative spend in a DO, hard-stop at 9,500/day (500 below the 10,000 limit), return `503 + Retry-After` when hit.
learningNotes: The DO `/try-debit` endpoint returns HTTP 200 with `allowed: false` (not 429) — the `503` surfaces only after the caller invokes `buildBudgetExhaustedResponse()` (`neuron-budget.ts:176`). Token-to-Neuron estimates carry a 20% buffer (`neuron-budget.ts:26`, `NEURON_BUFFER = 1.2`); the 500-unit headroom absorbs estimation error, not traffic spikes.

If the DO cold-starts mid-request the in-memory cache is empty, so the first consume re-reads from storage — correct but adds one extra storage read per cold start.

items.title: Vectors & Vector Spaces
items.summary: A vector is a point and an arrow. The dot product measures alignment (how much one vector projects onto another); orthogonality means zero projection. Most ML objects — embeddings, weights, activations — are vectors living in some space.

reviewQuestions.question: What does the dot product measure geometrically?
reviewQuestions.answer: The dot product measures how much one vector projects onto another — alignment. u·v = ||u|| ||v|| cos θ. Zero dot product means orthogonal (perpendicular) vectors.

items.title: Matrices & Linear Transformations
items.summary: A matrix is a machine that takes vectors in and pushes vectors out. Columns tell you where the basis vectors land. Matrix multiply chains machines — output of one feeds the next.

reviewQuestions.question: How do the columns of a matrix relate to a linear transformation?
reviewQuestions.answer: Each column is where a basis vector lands after the transformation. Matrix multiplication composes transformations: the columns of AB are A applied to the columns of B.

items.title: Probability Fundamentals
items.summary: Probability is a bookkeeping system for uncertainty. Conditional probability restricts the sample space; Bayes flips cause and effect — from 'how likely is the evidence given the hypothesis' to 'how likely is the hypothesis given the evidence'.

reviewQuestions.question: State Bayes' rule and when you need it.
reviewQuestions.answer: P(A|B) = P(B|A)·P(A) / P(B). Use it when you have a likelihood and a prior but need the posterior — e.g. updating disease probability after a test result.

reviewQuestions.question: How is independence different from mutual exclusivity?
reviewQuestions.answer: Mutually exclusive events cannot both happen (P(A∩B)=0). Independent events can co-occur, and P(A∩B)=P(A)P(B). Independence is about information; exclusivity is about overlap.

items.title: Descriptive Statistics
items.summary: Descriptive stats compress a dataset into a few numbers you can reason about. Mean chases outliers; median resists them. Correlation measures linear co-movement, not causation — and breaks under nonlinearity.

reviewQuestions.question: When should you report median instead of mean?
reviewQuestions.answer: When the distribution is skewed or has heavy tails (latency, income, error rates). The median resists outliers; the mean gets pulled by them and misrepresents the typical value.

reviewQuestions.question: Why does correlation not imply causation?
reviewQuestions.answer: Correlation only measures linear association. A third confounding variable, reverse causality, or coincidence can produce high r without A causing B.

items.title: Derivatives & Gradients
items.summary: The gradient points uphill steepest. To minimize a loss, walk opposite the gradient. Each partial derivative tells you how sensitive the output is to one input — the chain rule chains these sensitivities through composed functions.

reviewQuestions.question: Why does gradient descent step opposite the gradient?
reviewQuestions.answer: The gradient points in the direction of steepest increase. To minimize a loss, move in the opposite direction — the steepest decrease — scaled by a learning rate.

items.title: ML Math Foundations
items.summary: Three lenses keep coming back: linear algebra describes the data (vectors and matrices), calculus describes learning (gradients), probability describes uncertainty. Pick the lens that matches the question.

reviewQuestions.question: Explain ML Math Foundations in your own words. What problem does it solve and where is it used?
reviewQuestions.answer: Three lenses keep coming back: linear algebra describes the data (vectors and matrices), calculus describes learning (gradients), probability describes uncertainty. Pick the lens that matches the question.

items.title: Gradient Descent
items.summary: Gradient descent is repeated tiny adjustments. At each step you move the weights a small amount in the direction that lowers the loss. The "learning rate" is how big each step is — too big and you overshoot the answer, too small and training crawls.

reviewQuestions.answer: Gradient descent is repeated tiny adjustments. At each step you move the weights a small amount in the direction that lowers the loss. The "learning rate" is how big each step is — too big and you overshoot the answer, too small and training crawls.

items.title: Backpropagation
items.summary: Backpropagation is how the network learns: compute the error at the output, then push that error backwards through each layer using the chain rule from calculus. You reuse values from the forward pass so you do not have to recompute them.

reviewQuestions.question: Explain Backpropagation in your own words. What problem does it solve and where is it used?
reviewQuestions.answer: Backpropagation is how the network learns: compute the error at the output, then push that error backwards through each layer using the chain rule from calculus. You reuse values from the forward pass so you do not have to recompute them.

items.title: Softmax & Cross-Entropy
items.summary: Softmax turns raw scores (logits) into probabilities that sum to 1. Cross-entropy then measures how wrong those probabilities are versus the true answer. Together they give a clean gradient that backprop can work with.

reviewQuestions.question: Explain Softmax & Cross-Entropy in your own words. What problem does it solve and where is it used?
reviewQuestions.answer: Softmax turns raw scores (logits) into probabilities that sum to 1. Cross-entropy then measures how wrong those probabilities are versus the true answer. Together they give a clean gradient that backprop can work with.

items.title: AdamW Optimizer
items.summary: AdamW is the standard optimizer for training transformers. It adapts the learning rate per parameter (Adam) and applies weight decay separately from the gradient (the "W"). The default choice unless you have a reason to switch.

reviewQuestions.question: Explain AdamW Optimizer in your own words. What problem does it solve and where is it used?

items.title: Tokenization (LLM)
items.summary: LLM tokenizers (BPE, SentencePiece) split text into small reusable pieces — often subwords — so the model can handle any input even if it has not seen the exact word. The model and the tokenizer are a pair; swapping one breaks the other.

items.title: Language Modeling
items.summary: A language model does one thing: predict the next token given the ones before it. Chat, code, reasoning — all of it is built on top of this single objective.

items.title: Sampling & Decoding
items.summary: After the model outputs a probability for each next token, you have to pick one. Greedy picks the top — safe but dull. Temperature, top-k, and top-p (nucleus) introduce controlled randomness. Pick by how much variety the task can tolerate.

items.title: Embeddings (Transformer)
items.summary: Embeddings turn discrete things (words, items, users) into vectors of numbers so that similar things end up close together in space. Distance and direction in that space carry meaning — that geometry is what the model learns from.

items.title: Self-Attention
items.summary: Self-attention lets every token look at every other token and pull in what is relevant. Each token emits a query, a key, and a value; the query-key dot products (scaled, softmaxed) become weights over the values. Causal masking blocks a token from seeing the future.

items.title: Multi-Head Attention
items.summary: Attention lets each token in a sentence look at every other token and weight how relevant each one is. "Multi-head" runs several of these attention passes in parallel so the model can track different kinds of relationships at once (e.g. grammar, references, position).

items.title: Transformer Block
items.summary: A transformer block is two sublayers — multi-head attention then a position-wise MLP — each wrapped in a residual connection and layer norm. Residuals keep gradients flowing; pre-LayerNorm (norm before the sublayer) makes deep stacks train stably.

items.summary: A training loop is forward pass → loss → backward pass → optimizer step, repeated over batches of data. When training looks broken, the data is the usual culprit (leaked, mislabeled, wrong shape) — check it before blaming the model.

items.title: Checkpointing

items.summary: LoRA fine-tunes a big model cheaply by adding small trainable matrices alongside the frozen weights. You only update around 1% of the parameters yet get most of the quality of full fine-tuning, and you can swap adapters at inference time.

items.title: RL Alignment (GRPO & Policy Gradient)
items.summary: Post-training RL treats the LM as a policy: sample completions, score them with a reward model, then nudge token probabilities toward high-reward outputs. GRPO compares completions within a group (relative advantages) and reuses rollouts with importance sampling + clipping so training stays stable off-policy.

resources.title: Policy Gradient for LMs — RL101 notes (Hamish Ivison)
Stanford CS336 — L15 Alignment: SFT & RLHF (Spring 2025)

reviewQuestions.question: Explain GRPO-style RL alignment in your own words. Why use group-relative advantages and importance sampling?
reviewQuestions.answer: The LM is a policy: sample several completions per prompt, score them, and push token probabilities toward better ones. Group-relative advantages (reward minus group mean, scaled by std) tell the model which completion beat its siblings on the same query. Importance sampling corrects for reusing rollouts from an older policy when training off-policy, and clipping keeps updates stable.

items.title: FSRS Retrievability Decay
items.summary: The formula `R = (1 + elapsed / (9 * S))^-1` describes how memory fades between reviews — codebase implements this in `decayConfidence()` (`src/lib/fsrs.ts:74-76`).
learningNotes: ts-fsrs 5.3.2 ships FSRS v5+ which uses a trainable decay exponent (`R = (1 + factor·t/S)^-w₂₀`), not the fixed `-1` exponent. The project's `decayConfidence` still uses the v4 approximation — accurate enough for display, but not bitwise-identical to what the scheduler itself computes.

items.title: WebAssembly.Memory patching for safety
items.summary: Monkey-patching `WebAssembly.Memory.prototype.grow` inside a Worker to hard-cap allocations before the Go runtime sees them.
learningNotes: `installMemoryCap()` (`src/lib/goWasmWorker.ts:54-73`) wraps `WebAssembly.Memory.prototype.grow` with a 4096-page (256 MiB) ceiling. It's idempotent via `proto.__capped` guard and runs before `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming` (`src/lib/goWasmWorker.ts:80,92`) so the Go runtime's own memory is covered. Overflow throws a `RangeError`, not a tab kill.

assessments.question: What is the recommended focus when studying “The First Law of Complexodynamics”?
options: Treat it as intuition-building rather than a formal ML result.
Focus on the tradeoff curves and assumptions. Later scaling work revises details, but this sets the mindset.
Read for the data collection and reward-model loop before later assistant-scale versions.
Track where human preference wins and where reward hacking or evaluation gaps can enter.
assessments.explanation: Complexity intuition

items.title: Quantifying the Rise and Fall of Complexity in Closed Systems: The Coffee Automaton
items.summary: Formalizes a small model for understanding how apparent complexity can emerge and dissipate.

assessments.question: What is the recommended focus when studying “Quantifying the Rise and Fall of Complexity in Closed Systems: The Coffee Automaton”?
options: Read for the data collection and reward-model loop before later assistant-scale versions.
Track where human preference wins and where reward hacking or evaluation gaps can enter.
Compare model size versus alignment data. Smaller aligned models can beat larger base models on preferred behavior.
Read after the essay. The value is the formal move from story to measurable complexity.
assessments.explanation: Complexity toy model

items.title: Variational Lossy Autoencoder
items.summary: Separates lossy representation from nuisance detail in a variational autoencoder setting.

assessments.question: What is the recommended focus when studying “Variational Lossy Autoencoder”?
options: Use it as a modern ML bridge back to the core question: what should a representation preserve or discard?
Track where human preference wins and where reward hacking or evaluation gaps can enter.
Compare model size versus alignment data. Smaller aligned models can beat larger base models on preferred behavior.
Focus on what the constitution controls and what still depends on human choices.
assessments.explanation: Representation and compression
